title: Using Mapping Files
text: Using Mapping Files Typical situations for using mapping files occur when you need to restructure an entity by adding or removing fields, changing definitions, and so on. Example: Changing and Adding Fields When converting data, you can change or add field definitions. For example, suppose you have an entity named TRACK that contains two fields, TRACK_ID and TRACK_NAME. In the past, 25 characters were sufficient for TRACK_NAME, but your requirements now indicate that 40 characters are needed for TRACK_NAME, along with a new field, TRACK_LOCATION, that contains 40 characters. Copy the data from the table or file associated with the current TRACK entity to an XML file: /cpy def:track.tracker xml: Copy This creates a file named track.xml that contains the current definitions and data for the entity TRACK. Modify the definitions in the application model. In this example, increase the size of TRACK_NAME and add a new field TRACK_LOCATION. Drop the old TRACK table from your DBMS. If necessary, create the TRACK table in your DBMS. For more information, see Create Table Facility . Create a mapping file, for example: ; track.map #map track.tracker track.tracker * ; copy all fields Copy This file maps fields of the same name from the old to the new TRACK entity. Use this mapping file to copy the data from the original TRACK entity (in the XML file) into the table or file with the new definitions. For example: /cpy xml:track.xml def: track.map Copy Example: Splitting One Field into Two When converting data, you can split one field into two. Suppose the original TRACK_NAME field sometimes contains the data that belongs in the TRACK_LOCATION field, that is, it contains the name and location, separated by a comma ( , ). You can use field mapping instructions to extract the data from the old TRACK_NAME field into the new TRACK_NAME and TRACK_LOCATION fields: ; track2.map #map track.tracker = track.tracker track_id track_id track_name track_name[<,] track_location track_name[>,] Copy Using this mapping file, if the original TRACK_NAME field contained 'Ascot,England', the new TRACK_NAME field would contain all the characters before the comma ('Ascot'), while the TRACK_LOCATION field would contain the characters after the comma ('England'). If the original TRACK_NAME field did not contain a comma, the new TRACK_LOCATION field would be empty while the new TRACK_NAME would contain the original data. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
